user_goals_summary: The user values direct, confident internal communication that centers the reader's experience and avoids performative sentiment. They prioritize precise action items with clear ownership, careful information sequencing, and a tone that respects the audience's intelligence and time.

rubric.name: Tonal Discipline
rubric.description: No motivational-poster language or sentimental closings. Emotional beats never appear where the reader is ready to act. The email ends on momentum — a clean handoff, a next step, or a forward-looking line — not a pat on the back.

dimensions.label: Free of sentimental, motivational-poster phrasing that reads as hollow
Closing is a clean handoff (action, contact info, or forward-looking line) — not emotional
Emotional language does not appear at points where the reader is ready to act or move on
Decisions are named as deliberate choices, not wrapped in excessive apology or hedging

rubric.name: Audience-Centric Framing
rubric.description: Impact is framed in terms of how it affects the reader's work, plans, and experience — not the organization's reputation or management's concerns. The reader is the subject of 'why this matters' reasoning.

dimensions.label: Consequences are framed in terms of how they affect the reader's work, schedule, or plans
Avoids centering the organization's reputation or management's perspective as the reason something matters
Acknowledges reader impact through concrete details, not abstract sentiment
The reader — not the company — is the subject of 'why this matters' framing

rubric.name: Actionable Specificity
rubric.description: Every ask has a named owner and a timeframe. No vague group instructions that nobody owns. If someone reads an action item, they know exactly who does what by when.

dimensions.label: Action items identify a specific person or role responsible
Asks include deadlines or timeframes
Each ask describes a specific, doable action — not a vague posture like 'stand by'
No group-wide instructions that lack a single point of accountability

rubric.name: Information Sequencing
rubric.description: The core message is delivered early — the lede is never buried. Emotional acknowledgment of impact lands before the difficult news, not after it as a footnote. Information flows in the order readers need to cognitively and emotionally process it.

dimensions.label: The core message or decision is delivered early and clearly
Acknowledgment of impact on the reader precedes the difficult information
The rationale is delivered close to the decision, not separated from it
Information is ordered to match how readers process the situation (context → news → implications → action)

rubric.name: Directness and Transparency
rubric.description: Bad news is delivered clearly and promptly, not softened into ambiguity. Reasoning is stated openly. The tone is professional but human — no panic signals, no clinical detachment.

dimensions.label: Difficult information is stated clearly, not hedged into ambiguity
The rationale behind decisions is stated openly rather than omitted or implied
Tone is professional but still human and approachable
Language does not signal uncertainty or panic through excessive hedging or apology

rubric.name: Scannability
rubric.description: Key information is broken into structured, scannable formats — bullets, bold labels, clear sections. A busy reader can extract takeaways in a single pass without reading every line.

dimensions.label: Multi-part information uses bullets, headers, or numbered lists
Important details are visually distinct through formatting (bold, bullets, etc.)
Each section or paragraph has a single clear purpose and doesn't mix concerns
A busy reader can extract key takeaways and action items in a single scan

coaching_notes: This user has a strong aversion to performative emotional language — anything that reads as hollow or sentimental will actively undermine the email's credibility in their eyes. They consistently evaluate from the reader's chair: every framing choice, acknowledgment, and action item must pass the test 'does this serve the person reading it?' They prefer precision over warmth and will trade a softer tone for a clearer one every time.

rubric.name: Information Sequencing
rubric.description: The email follows a strict arc: acknowledge the situation or effort first (one sentence max), then deliver the news, then explain why it matters, then one concrete next step. Violating this order — even if all elements are present — breaks the flow. This is non-negotiable.

dimensions.label: Opens with a brief (one sentence max) acknowledgment of the situation or effort — not the news
The core message or decision is delivered after the acknowledgment, not buried further down
The 'why it matters' explanation follows the news — not before it, not after the CTA
One concrete next step closes the body — stated directly, with no setup clause before it and no scaffolding after it
All four elements appear in sequence: acknowledge → news → stakes → next step

rubric.name: Audience-Centric Framing
rubric.description: Impact is framed in terms of how it affects the reader's work, plans, and experience — not the organization's reputation or management's concerns. The reader is the subject of 'why this matters' reasoning. Drafts that center the sender's logic feel tone-deaf.

dimensions.label: Consequences are framed in terms of how they affect the reader's work, schedule, or plans
Avoids centering the organization's reputation or management's perspective as the reason something matters
Acknowledges reader impact through concrete details, not abstract sentiment
The reader — not the company — is the subject of 'why this matters' framing

rubric.name: Actionable Specificity
rubric.description: Every ask has one named owner and a timeframe. No group instructions that nobody owns. If multiple people need to do different things, the email designates a single point of accountability — but doesn't over-specify org structure details that the email has no business assuming.

dimensions.label: Action items identify a specific person or role responsible — not a group
Asks include deadlines or timeframes
Each ask describes a specific, doable action — not a vague posture like 'stay tuned' or 'please review'
No group-wide instructions that lack a single point of accountability
Action items don't assume org structure details or internal processes the email shouldn't contain

rubric.name: Tonal Discipline
rubric.description: No motivational-poster language, sentimental closings, setup framing before the CTA, or trailing sentiment after it. The email ends on momentum — a clean handoff or forward-looking line. The call to action stands alone: nothing before it to warm it up, nothing after it to soften the landing.

dimensions.label: Free of sentimental, motivational-poster phrasing — and functional-sounding filler ('To get ready for kickoff…') that reads as motivational padding even when it isn't overtly emotional
Closing is a clean handoff (action, contact info, or forward-looking line) — not emotional
No setup framing ('To get ready for…') before the action, and no trailing sentiment after it — the CTA stands alone
Action items are stated directly without introductory framing clauses that don't add information
Decisions are named as deliberate choices, not wrapped in excessive apology or hedging

rubric.name: Directness and Transparency
rubric.description: Bad news is delivered clearly and promptly, not softened into ambiguity. Reasoning is stated openly. The tone is professional but human — no panic signals, no corporate detachment, no jargon.

dimensions.label: Difficult information is stated clearly, not hedged into ambiguity
The rationale behind decisions is stated openly rather than omitted or implied
Tone is professional but still human and approachable
Free of corporate jargon, buzzwords, and filler phrases — prefer plain verbs ('flag,' 'send,' 'confirm') over elevated substitutes ('surface,' 'socialize,' 'align on')

rubric.name: Scannability
rubric.description: Short paragraphs. Key information is easy to extract in a single pass. A busy reader doesn't need to read every line to get the point and know what to do.

dimensions.label: Paragraphs are short — no walls of text
Multi-part information uses bullets, headers, or numbered lists where appropriate
Each paragraph has a single clear purpose and doesn't mix concerns
A busy reader can extract key takeaways and action items in a single scan

user_goals_summary: The user values direct, confident internal communication that centers the reader's experience and avoids performative sentiment. They prioritize precise action items with clear ownership, careful information sequencing, and a tone that respects the audience's intelligence and time.
coaching_notes: This user has a strong aversion to performative emotional language — anything that reads as hollow or sentimental will actively undermine the email's credibility in their eyes. They consistently evaluate from the reader's chair: every framing choice, acknowledgment, and action item must pass the test 'does this serve the person reading it?' They prefer precision over warmth and will trade a softer tone for a clearer one every time.

writing_type: internal team communications and stakeholder emails
user_goals_summary: Communicates decisions and updates to technical teams with a strict information arc (acknowledge → news → stakes → next step), audience-centric framing that centers the reader's work and plans, and a clean closing that points forward. Values rhythmic precision — how sentences land matters as much as whether the right content is present.

rubric.name: Information Sequencing
rubric.description: The email follows a strict arc: acknowledge the situation or effort first (one sentence max), then deliver the news, then explain why it matters, then one concrete next step. The acknowledgment and the news must not collapse into one continuous thought — there must be a clear rhythmic break between them. Violating this order or compressing these beats breaks the flow.

dimensions.label: Opens with a single-sentence acknowledgment of the reader's situation or effort — not the news itself
The acknowledgment and the news are clearly separated — they don't flow into each other as one continuous thought
The 'why it matters' explanation follows the news — not before it, not after the CTA
One concrete next step closes the body — pointing forward to what happens next, not trailing off into a passive redirect or explanation
All four elements appear in sequence: acknowledge → news → stakes → next step

rubric.name: Audience-Centric Framing
rubric.description: Impact is framed in terms of how it affects the reader's work, plans, and experience — not the organization's reputation or management's concerns. The reader is the subject of 'why this matters' reasoning. Drafts that center the sender's logic feel tone-deaf.

dimensions.label: Consequences are framed in terms of how they affect the reader's work, schedule, or plans
Avoids centering the organization's reputation or management's perspective as the reason something matters
Acknowledges reader impact through concrete details (e.g., naming what's been disrupted), not abstract sentiment
The reader — not the company — is the grammatical and logical subject of 'why this matters' framing

rubric.name: Actionable Specificity
rubric.description: Every ask has one named owner and a timeframe. No group instructions that nobody owns. If a section presents one next step, it has one owner — don't split attention across multiple people doing different things in the same section. Doesn't over-specify org structure details the email has no business assuming.

dimensions.label: Action items identify a specific person or role responsible — not a group
Asks include deadlines or timeframes
Each ask describes a specific, doable action — not a vague posture like 'stay tuned' or 'please review'
Each discrete next step has a single owner — the closing doesn't split attention across multiple people doing different things
Action items don't assume org structure details or internal processes the email shouldn't contain

rubric.name: Tonal Discipline
rubric.description: No motivational-poster language, sentimental closings, or trailing sentiment after the CTA. The email ends on momentum — a forward-pointing action or handoff, not a passive 'questions go to' redirect or a pat on the back. Anything after the action item weakens it.

dimensions.label: Free of sentimental, motivational-poster phrasing and functional-sounding filler that adds no information
Closing is a forward-pointing action or handoff — not a passive contact-info redirect or sentimental note
No trailing sentiment, helpdesk redirects, or scaffolding after the final action item — the CTA stands alone
Action items are stated directly without introductory framing clauses that don't add information
Decisions are named as deliberate choices, not wrapped in excessive apology or hedging

rubric.description: Bad news is delivered clearly and promptly, not softened into ambiguity. Reasoning is stated openly. The tone is professional but human — no panic signals, no corporate detachment, no jargon.

dimensions.label: Difficult information is stated clearly, not hedged into ambiguity

dimensions.label: Paragraphs are short — no walls of text

coaching_notes: This user has an unusually precise sense of rhythm and pacing — they distinguish between content being logically correct and content 'landing right.' They will catch when two beats collapse into one thought even if all information is present. Closings are a consistent friction point: anything that feels like it's settling, redirecting passively, or adding sentiment after the action will get flagged. When they say 'one owner,' they mean it literally — splitting accountability across two people in the same section reads as ambiguity, not thoroughness.

user_goals_summary: Writes internal team communications that follow a strict acknowledge → news → stakes → action arc with clear rhythmic separation between beats. Values audience-centric framing that centers the reader's work and plans rather than organizational logic. Demands frictionless, single-owner action items and closings that point forward rather than trailing off into sentiment or passive redirects.

coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: the acknowledge → news → stakes → action arc with rhythmic separation. This user will revise multiple rounds until beats don't bleed together. Trade-off preference: when in doubt, reduce friction for the reader — put the onus on the sender's side rather than asking the reader to coordinate. This user notices closing lines that feel passive or sentimental immediately and treats them as structural failures, not stylistic quibbles.
